eras.name: Ancient Textile Kingdoms
eras.description: The Javanese courts of Majapahit and Mataram developed batik as a sacred art form tied to Hindu-Buddhist cosmology. Each motif carried spiritual significance, and certain patterns were restricted to royalty through sumptuary laws.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Javanese court dancer from the Majapahit era wearing a finely drawn batik kain panjang in indigo and brown soga dyes, gold kemben chest wrap, elaborate gold crown and jewelry, temple relief carvings in background.

eras.name: Spice Trade and Textile Exchange
eras.description: Indonesia's position at the center of global spice routes brought Indian patola cloth, Chinese silk, and Arab textiles, creating a rich fusion. Sumatra's weavers developed elaborate gold-threaded songket to compete with imported luxury.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 16th-century Sumatran queen in a Minangkabau court wearing a shimmering gold songket sarong with supplementary weft patterns, a silk baju kurung, and towering gold filigree headdress, tropical palace setting.

eras.name: Colonial Fusion and Nyonya Style
eras.description: Dutch colonial rule created hybrid fashion identities. Peranakan (Nyonya) women developed exquisite beaded slippers and kebaya styles, while Javanese court batik reached its artistic peak under the sultanates of Yogyakarta and Solo.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Peranakan Nyonya woman in 1920s Semarang wearing a delicate white lace kebaya over a fine tulis batik sarong in the parang pattern, intricate beaded slippers, kerosang brooches at the neckline.

eras.name: National Identity and Batik Diplomacy
eras.description: After independence, batik was elevated to a symbol of Indonesian nationhood. President Sukarno popularized batik shirts in diplomatic settings, while UNESCO recognized batik as Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2009.
eras.aiImagePrompt: President Sukarno era 1960s fashion: an Indonesian diplomat in a crisply tailored batik shirt with parang motif in brown and cream, standing in a mid-century modern government building.

eras.name: The Modest Fashion Revolution
eras.description: Indonesia emerged as the global capital of Muslim modest fashion. Designers transformed the hijab from a simple covering into a high-fashion statement, creating a multi-billion dollar industry serving 200 million consumers.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A contemporary Indonesian modest fashion editorial: a model in an elegant flowing pastel abaya-inspired gown with intricate batik-printed hijab, modern architectural mosque interior with geometric light patterns.

eras.name: Jakarta Street Style and Digital Fashion
eras.description: Jakarta's youth have created a vibrant streetwear scene blending Japanese, Korean, and local influences. Social media has democratized fashion, making Indonesia one of the world's most influential markets for digital-first brands.
eras.aiImagePrompt: Jakarta street style photography: a young Indonesian creative wearing an oversized vintage batik shirt layered over a graphic tee, wide-leg pants, chunky sneakers, standing in a neon-lit South Jakarta alley.

textiles.name: Batik Tulis
textiles.description: Hand-drawn wax-resist dyed cloth where molten wax is applied with a canting (pen-like tool) to create intricate patterns before dyeing. A single cloth can take months to complete.
textiles.technique: Hand-drawn wax resist applied with copper canting tool, dipped in natural or synthetic dyes, wax removed by boiling

textiles.name: Songket
textiles.description: A luxurious supplementary-weft brocade woven with gold or silver metallic threads, traditionally worn for weddings and royal ceremonies across Sumatra, Bali, and Kalimantan.
textiles.technique: Supplementary weft weaving on a backstrap loom with metallic threads inserted by hand between base weft rows

textiles.name: Ikat Tenun
textiles.description: A resist-dyed woven cloth where patterns are created by tying and dyeing the warp or weft threads before weaving, producing characteristic blurred-edge motifs.
textiles.technique: Warp threads are tied in bundles and resist-dyed before being set on a backstrap loom for weaving

textiles.name: Gringsing (Double Ikat)
textiles.description: One of the world's rarest textiles, where both warp and weft threads are resist-dyed before weaving, producing geometrically precise sacred patterns. Only produced in one village.
textiles.technique: Both warp and weft threads are individually tied and dyed multiple times, then aligned precisely on the loom

textiles.name: Ulos
textiles.description: Sacred handwoven cloth of the Batak people, given as gifts at important life ceremonies. Different ulos patterns carry specific social and spiritual meanings.
textiles.technique: Backstrap loom weaving with supplementary weft patterning using hand-spun cotton

climate.fashionImplication: Year-round heat and humidity demand breathable natural fibers like cotton and silk. Loose, flowing silhouettes such as the sarong and kebaya are practical staples. The monsoon season influences the dominance of easy-wash, quick-dry fabrics.

culturalInfluences: Hindu-Buddhist temple art and Javanese court ritual
Islamic modesty principles and Middle Eastern trade
Japanese and Korean pop culture influence
300+ ethnic groups with distinct textile traditions
The concept of 'adat' (customary law) governing dress codes

emergingTrends: Modest fashion as global luxury export
